items.title: Essential Nutrients and Their Role in Health Maintenance
items.content: Nutrients are vital substances that the body cannot synthesize in sufficient amounts, necessitating dietary intake. These nutrients support growth, organ function, metabolism, nitrogen balance, and overall nutrient status. Essential nutrients include proteins (9 essential amino acids), fats (several fatty acids), carbohydrates (glucose), vitamins (4 fat-soluble and 10 water-soluble), minerals (4 major and 7 trace), electrolytes (3 types), dietary fiber, choline, and ultra-trace elements.
keyPoints: Nutrients are essential for growth, organ function, metabolism, nitrogen balance, and nutrient status.

Essential nutrients include: 9 amino acids, several fatty acids, glucose, 4 fat-soluble vitamins, 10 water-soluble vitamins, dietary fiber, choline, 4 minerals, 7 trace minerals, 3 electrolytes, and ultra-trace elements.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.title: Conditionally Essential Nutrients in Special Populations
items.content: Certain nutrients are conditionally essential for specific populations due to their inability to synthesize these compounds adequately. These groups include individuals with genetic defects, those experiencing infections or trauma, and some immature infants who cannot produce sufficient amounts of inositol, taurine, arginine, and/or glutamine.
keyPoints: Conditionally essential nutrients are required for specific populations unable to synthesize them adequately.
Populations include individuals with genetic defects, pathologies like infection or trauma, and some immature infants.
Examples of conditionally essential nutrients: inositol, taurine, arginine, glutamine.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.title: Understanding Energy Requirements for Weight Stability
items.content: Energy intake must match total energy expenditure to maintain weight stability. The estimated energy requirement (EER) is calculated based on factors such as age, sex, weight, height, and physical activity level.
keyPoints: Average energy intake: ~2600 kcal/d for American men, ~1800 kcal/d for American women.
REE formula: For males, REE = 900 + 10m; for females, REE = 700 + 7m (where m is mass in kg).
PAL ranges from 1.4 (inactive) to 2.05 (very active), with sedentary individuals often overestimating their activity levels.
EER provides a target for caloric needs based on individual characteristics.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Protein Requirements and Considerations
items.content: The recommended dietary allowance (RDA) for protein is ~0.8 g/kg of desirable body mass per day, assuming energy needs are met. Protein needs increase during growth, pregnancy, lactation, and recovery from injury.
keyPoints: Nine essential amino acids must be supplied by diet: histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine/cystine, phenylalanine/tyrosine, threonine, tryptophan, valine.
Protein provides 10–14% of calories in a healthy diet; most American diets meet this requirement.
Increased protein intake is necessary during energy deprivation to prevent protein-calorie malnutrition.
Tolerance to dietary protein decreases in renal insufficiency and liver failure.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Optimal Fat and Carbohydrate Intake for Health
items.content: Fat should constitute no more than 30% of calories, with specific limits on saturated, trans, and polyunsaturated fats. Carbohydrates should provide 45–55% of total calories.
keyPoints: Fats: ~34% of U.S. diet calories; limit saturated and trans fats to <10%, polyunsaturated fats to <10%.
Carbohydrates: Provide 45–55% of total calories, with <6% from added sugars.
Brain requires ~100 g of glucose per day; other tissues use ~50 g/d.
Adaptations during hypocaloric states can lower carbohydrate needs over time.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Water Requirements in Adults
items.content: Adults generally require 1–1.5 mL of water per kilocalorie of energy expenditure to maintain hydration under normal conditions, accounting for variations in physical activity and diet.
keyPoints: Normal adult water requirement: 1–1.5 mL/kcal of energy expenditure.
Water losses include 50–100 mL/d in feces, 500–1000 mL/d by evaporation or exhalation, ≥1000 mL/d in urine depending on renal solute load.
Increased external losses (e.g., fever, diarrhea) necessitate increased water intake to prevent underhydration.
Kidneys can excrete up to 18 L/day of excess water if intake is high and solute intake is adequate.
Obligatory urine output may compromise hydration with inadequate intake or increased losses.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.title: Water Needs in Special Populations
items.content: Infants, pregnant women, lactating mothers, and the elderly have unique water requirements due to physiological differences and external factors.
keyPoints: Infants require more water due to large surface area to volume ratios, inability to communicate thirst, and immature kidneys.
Increased water needs during pregnancy: ~30 mL/d.
Lactation requires ~1000 mL additional water daily or 1 mL for each milliliter of milk produced.
Elderly individuals have reduced total-body water, blunted thirst sensation, and may use diuretics affecting hydration.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Dietary Reference Intakes and Recommended Dietary Allowances
items.content: The DRIs, including the RDA, provide guidelines for nutrient intake to ensure health across various populations. The RDA is set two standard deviations above the EAR to cover nearly all healthy individuals' needs.
keyPoints: DRIs are used in the US and Canada to guide dietary planning and clinical practice.
RDA is set two standard deviations above the EAR, ensuring nutrient requirements for nearly all (~97-98%) of a specific population group.
The RDA represents an intake goal that exceeds actual needs for ~2–3% of the population.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.title: Role and Limitations of Estimated Average Requirement
items.content: The EAR is a median nutrient intake estimate for half of a specific group, indicating that individuals at this level have a 50% risk of inadequate intake.
keyPoints: EAR is the amount estimated to be adequate for 50% of healthy individuals in a specific age and sex group.
A person with usual intake at the EAR has a 50% risk of inadequate nutrient intake, making it less useful for individual assessment.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.title: Application of RDAs in Dietary Planning and Labeling
items.content: RDAs are utilized to create food guides, therapeutic diet plans, and nutritional labels, ensuring that dietary recommendations meet the needs of nearly all individuals.
keyPoints: RDAs guide the formulation of food guides like USDA's MyPlate Plan and food-exchange lists for therapeutic diets.
Nutritional content on food labels is expressed as a percentage of the daily value (DV), based on the highest RDA for an adult consuming 2000 kcal.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Adequate Intake (AI) and its Application
items.content: Adequate Intake (AI) is used when there is insufficient evidence to establish an Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) and thus no Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA). AIs are based on observed or experimentally determined approximations of nutrient intakes in healthy populations. They apply to infants up to age 1 year, as well as certain nutrients for all ages.
keyPoints: AI is used when an RDA cannot be set due to lack of EAR data.
AIs are proposed for specific nutrients including chromium, fluoride, manganese, sodium, potassium, pantothenic acid, biotin, choline, fiber, and water for all ages; and for infants up to age 1 year.
AI is based on observed or experimentally determined nutrient intakes in healthy people.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Tolerable Upper Levels (UL) and Chronic Disease Risk Reduction Intake (CDRR)
items.content: Tolerable Upper Levels (UL) represent the highest level of daily nutrient intake that is unlikely to pose a risk of adverse health effects for most people. The UL helps prevent toxicity from excessive nutrient intake, especially from fortified foods and supplements. Chronic Disease Risk Reduction Intake (CDRR) identifies intake levels above which reducing consumption can lower chronic disease risk.
keyPoints: UL is the highest level of daily nutrient intake unlikely to pose a health risk for most people.
No UL means lack of data, not absence of risk from high intake.
Sodium CDRR for adults is 2300 mg/d; this level is associated with reduced chronic disease risk.
CDRRs are not available for potassium or other nutrients.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.title: Increased Nutrient Needs During Physiological Changes
items.content: Physiological factors such as growth, strenuous physical activity, pregnancy, and lactation increase the need for energy and essential nutrients. Energy needs rise during pregnancy due to fetal growth demands and increased energy required for milk production during lactation.
keyPoints: Energy needs rise during pregnancy and lactation.
Loss of lean body mass decreases REE, reducing energy needs in older adults (>70 years).
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.title: Impact of Dietary Composition on Nutrient Availability
items.content: Dietary composition affects the biological availability and use of nutrients. For example, iron absorption may be impaired by large amounts of calcium or lead; non-heme iron uptake can be hindered without ascorbic acid and amino acids.
keyPoints: Iron absorption affected by dietary components like calcium and ascorbic acid.
Animal proteins have higher biological values compared to plant proteins, which may require combination with other sources for optimal nutrient use.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.title: Disease-Related Nutrient Deficiencies and Chronic Disease Risk Factors
items.content: Deficiency diseases such as protein-calorie malnutrition, iron-deficiency anemia, and vitamin deficiencies (e.g., vitamin D, B12) result from imbalances between nutrient supply and body needs. Excesses in certain nutrients are risk factors for chronic diseases like coronary artery disease and hypertension.
keyPoints: Deficiency diseases include conditions like rickets (vitamin D deficiency), scurvy (vitamin C deficiency).
Excessive intake of saturated fats, sodium, and ethanol is linked to chronic degenerative diseases.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.title: Nutrition Screening and Reassessment in Acute Care
items.content: In acute care settings, nutrition screening is essential to identify malnutrition risk within 24 hours of admission. The GLIM criteria include evaluating weight loss, BMI, muscle mass, food intake, and disease burden. Nutritional status should be reassessed at least weekly.
keyPoints: The Joint Commission requires nutrition screening within 24 hours of hospital admission.
GLIM criteria for acute care: evaluate weight loss, low BMI (<19 or >25), reduced muscle mass, reduced food intake/absorption, increased disease burden/inflammation.
Reassess nutritional status at least once every week.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Indicators of Inadequate Nutritional Intake in Acute-Care Settings
items.content: In acute-care settings, inadequate intake can be identified through observations such as refusal to eat, limited diet orders like clear liquids for extended periods, and conditions affecting the alimentary tract. Abnormal biochemical values (serum albumin <35 g/L, serum cholesterol <3.9 mmol/L) may indicate a need for further assessment.
keyPoints: Observations of inadequate intake: refusal to eat, limited diet orders, skipped meals due to tests/procedures.
Conditions affecting intake: fever, dehydration, gastrointestinal distress, vomiting, diarrhea, comatose state.
Abnormal biochemical values indicating potential malnutrition: serum albumin <35 g/L, serum cholesterol <3.9 mmol/L.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Nutritional Monitoring for High-Risk Patients
items.content: Patients with severe illness, long hospital stays, or specific diagnoses like gastrointestinal diseases, end-stage renal disease, and insulin-dependent diabetes require rigorous nutritional monitoring. Those on enteral/parenteral feeding or home chemotherapy also need specialized assessment by certified professionals.
keyPoints: High-risk patients: very ill, long hospital stays, discharged with GI tract issues, end-stage renal disease, insulin-dependent diabetes.
Specialized nutritional support needed for enteral/parenteral feeding and home chemotherapy.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Dietary Assessment in Ambulatory Settings
items.content: Dietary assessment in outpatient settings aims to evaluate if a patient's diet poses health risks, contributes to chronic disease issues, or necessitates medical nutrition therapy. It involves reviewing food intake adequacy and identifying dietary components affected by specific diagnoses and comorbidities.
keyPoints: Assessment includes usual food intakes, supplements, medications, and alcohol.
Tools like ASA24, DHQ, and Nutritools aid in dietary assessment.
Findings guide personalized dietary recommendations and therapeutic prescriptions.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Guidelines for Healthy Dietary Patterns
items.content: For individuals not requiring medical nutrition therapy, the USDA's Choose My Plate provides guidance to avoid inadequate or excessive nutrient intakes. It emphasizes a balanced diet with increased fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat milk while reducing sodium and sugary drinks.
keyPoints: Choose My Plate encourages balance between calories and nutritional needs.
Web-based calculator tailors servings based on weight, sex, age, and life-cycle stages.
Reviewing the guide helps patients adjust their dietary patterns for better health.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.title: When to Conduct a Full Nutritional Status Assessment
items.content: A comprehensive nutritional status assessment is indicated for seriously ill patients or those at very high nutritional risk when the cause of malnutrition remains uncertain after initial evaluations.
keyPoints: Reserved for seriously ill patients and those with very high nutritional risk.
Conducted when malnutrition's cause is unclear post-initial evaluation.
Involves dietary intake documentation, health history, anthropometric measurements, biochemical tests, clinical examination, and functional status.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.title: Global Considerations in Nutrient Recommendations
items.content: DRIs are global estimates for healthy individuals when adjusted for age, sex, body size, and activity. AIs, AMDRs, and CDRRs should be used cautiously outside their original context.
keyPoints: DRIs (EAR, UL, energy needs) apply globally with adjustments.
AIs are based on U.S. and Canadian population data.
AMDRs reflect expert opinion in North America.
CDRRs may vary across populations.
Global harmonization of nutrient recommendations is increasing.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.question: Which of the following is NOT considered an essential nutrient?
items.explanation: Pesticides are not classified as essential nutrients; they are bioactive compounds with potential health effects.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.question: Which of the following groups is NOT typically considered when determining nutrient requirements?
items.explanation: Nutrient requirements are determined for human populations, not genetically modified organisms.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.question: Which of the following nutrients is conditionally essential?
items.explanation: Arginine can be conditionally essential in certain individuals, such as those with specific genetic defects or developmental immaturity.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of total energy expenditure (TEE)?
items.explanation: Basal metabolic rate is not explicitly mentioned as a component of TEE in the passage.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What is the estimated energy requirement (EER) primarily used for?
options: To calculate total energy expenditure directly
To maintain energy balance and health in adults
items.explanation: The EER is the predicted average requirement to maintain energy balance and health.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following amino acids is considered essential?
items.explanation: Leucine is one of the nine essential amino acids.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of calories from protein is recommended for a healthy diet?
items.explanation: Current recommendations call for at least 10–14% of calories from protein.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended water intake per kilocalorie of energy expenditure under usual conditions for adults?
items.explanation: The passage states that 1–1.5 mL of water per kilocalorie is sufficient under usual conditions.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which condition increases water losses by approximately 200 mL/d for each degree Celsius rise?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that fever increases water losses by ~200 mL/d per °C.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: During lactation, how much additional daily water is needed to support milk production?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that ~1000 mL of additional water is needed during lactation.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary purpose of the Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) in dietary planning?
options: It provides a nutrient intake goal for nearly all healthy individuals.
It serves as a median requirement to estimate adequacy for half of a specific population group.
It sets an upper limit on nutrient intake to prevent toxicity.
It determines the minimum daily intake necessary to avoid deficiency diseases.
items.explanation: The EAR is the amount of a nutrient estimated to be adequate for half of the healthy individuals in a specific group, serving as a median requirement.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.question: Which statement best describes how the RDA is determined?
options: It is set at two standard deviations below the EAR.
It represents the nutrient intake for 50% of a population group.
It is set two standard deviations above the EAR to meet nearly all individuals' needs.
It is based solely on clinical deficiency signs.
items.explanation: The RDA is set two standard deviations above the EAR to ensure that the nutrient requirements of nearly all healthy persons are met.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.question: What does the term 'tolerable upper level' (UL) refer to in the context of DRIs?
options: The minimum nutrient intake required for maintaining health.
The highest level of daily nutrient intake that is likely to pose no risk of adverse effects.
The average requirement for half of a population group.
A guideline used exclusively for therapeutic diet planning.
items.explanation: The UL refers to the highest level of daily nutrient intake that is likely to pose no risk of adverse health effects.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.question: Why might individuals with severe comorbidities or specific medications require different dietary reference intakes?
options: They have the same nutrient requirements as the general population.
Their energy and nutrient needs may be altered by their conditions or medications.
DRIs are not applicable to individuals with chronic diseases.
Only macronutrient distribution ranges need adjustment for these individuals.
items.explanation: Individuals with severe comorbidities or specific medications might require different DRIs because their energy and nutrient requirements may be altered by their conditions or medications.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.question: Which nutrient intake level is used when an RDA cannot be set due to a lack of an EAR?
items.explanation: Adequate Intake (AI) is used when there is no Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) to establish an RDA.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.question: What does the Tolerable Upper Level (UL) represent?
options: The minimum nutrient intake level required for health.
The highest level of daily nutrient intake unlikely to pose a risk of adverse effects.
The recommended daily allowance for nutrients.
The amount of nutrient intake that reduces chronic disease risk.
items.explanation: UL is the highest level of chronic nutrient intake unlikely to pose a risk of adverse health effects.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.question: For which nutrients are AIs proposed instead of RDAs for all age groups?
items.explanation: AIs are proposed for chromium, fluoride, manganese, sodium, potassium, pantothenic acid, biotin, choline, fiber, and water.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for the decrease in energy needs of older persons?
items.explanation: Energy needs decrease with loss of lean body mass, which is the major determinant of REE.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.question: What dietary component can impair non-heme iron absorption?
items.explanation: Non-heme iron uptake may be impaired by a lack of ascorbic acid and amino acids in the meal.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.question: Which nutrient's oral bioavailability is often only half that obtained by parenteral administration?
items.explanation: The oral bioavailability of most mineral elements may be only half that obtained by parenteral administration.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.question: Which chronic degenerative disease is associated with excess intake of saturated/trans-fat and cholesterol?
items.explanation: Saturated/trans-fat and cholesterol are recognized as risk factors for coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.question: Which organization requires most health care facilities to perform nutrition screening within 24 hours after hospital admission?
items.explanation: The Joint Commission accredits and certifies health care organizations in the United States, requiring nutrition screening within 24 hours after admission.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is a key component of the GLIM tool used for evaluating malnutrition risk in acute care settings?
options: Evaluation of weight loss and reduced muscle mass
items.explanation: The GLIM tool evaluates weight loss, low BMI, reduced muscle mass, among other factors to assess malnutrition risk.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: In acute-care settings, what is the primary goal of dietary assessment?
options: To identify and avoid inadequate intake
To ensure patient satisfaction with meals
items.explanation: The goal in acute-care settings is to identify and avoid inadequate intake, assure appropriate intake, and determine if malnutrition is due to poor dietary intake or other causes.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions may necessitate special nutritional assessment and monitoring?
items.explanation: Patients with end-stage renal disease require special nutritional assessment and monitoring due to their complex dietary needs.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which tool is a web-based, self-administered, 24-hour recall used for dietary assessment?
items.explanation: The Automated Self-Administered 24-Hour Dietary Assessment Tool (ASA24) is specifically mentioned as a web-based, self-administered, 24-hour recall tool.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary aim of dietary assessment in an outpatient setting?
options: To determine if the patient requires surgery
To assess the adequacy of present and usual food intakes
To prescribe medications for chronic diseases
items.explanation: The aim is to determine whether or not the patient's diet poses a health risk, contributes to chronic disease-related problems, and if medical nutrition therapy is needed.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which organization provides therapeutic dietary prescriptions for most diseases?
options: National Institute of Health's National Cancer Institute
items.explanation: Therapeutic dietary prescriptions are available in the diet manuals of most hospitals and from the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.question: Which components are involved in a full nutritional status assessment for seriously ill patients?
options: Documentation of dietary intake, health history, anthropometric measurements, biochemical measurements, clinical examination, and functional status evaluation.
Only documentation of dietary intake and health history.
Anthropometric measurements and biochemical measurements only.
Clinical examination and functional status evaluation without other assessments.
items.explanation: A full nutritional status assessment involves multiple dimensions including dietary intake, health history, anthropometric and biochemical measurements, clinical examination, and functional status evaluation.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.question: Which of the following DRIs are based on experimental evidence?
options: AIs (Adequate Intakes) and AMDRs (Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Ranges).
EAR (Estimated Average Requirement), UL (Tolerable Upper Intake Level), and energy needs.
items.explanation: The EAR, UL, and energy needs are DRIs based on experimental evidence.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.question: Which organization has developed nutrient-based standards similar to the DRIs?
options: World Health Organization/Food and Agricultural Organization of the United Nations.
items.explanation: Both the World Health Organization/Food and Agricultural Organization of the United Nations and the European Food Safety Authority have developed nutrient-based standards similar to the DRIs.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.statement: The absence of essential nutrients can lead to growth impairment and organ dysfunction.
items.explanation: Lack of essential nutrients results in various health issues, including growth impairment and organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.statement: All individuals require the same amounts of nutrients regardless of age or physiological state.
items.explanation: Individual nutrient requirements vary based on factors such as age and physiological state.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.statement: The thermic effect of food is a major component of total energy expenditure.
items.explanation: The thermic effect of food is considered a minor component of TEE.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: In healthy individuals, the timing of protein intake throughout the day significantly affects protein requirements.
items.explanation: The passage states that the timing of protein intake has little effect in healthy people with adequate diets.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Elderly individuals have increased total-body water compared to younger adults.
items.explanation: The passage states that the elderly have reduced total-body water.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Normal kidneys can excrete up to 18 L of excess water per day.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that the kidneys can adjust by excreting up to 18 L of excess water per day.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: The RDA is a suitable criterion for evaluating individual nutrient adequacy.
items.explanation: The RDA exceeds the actual requirements of all but ~2–3% of the population, making it overly generous for assessing individual adequacy.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.statement: Energy has a Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) and Tolerable Upper Level (UL).
items.explanation: Energy does not have an RDA or UL; instead, Estimated Energy Requirements (EERs) are used.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.statement: Nutrient levels in commonly eaten foods rarely exceed the Tolerable Upper Level.
items.explanation: Commonly eaten foods seldom surpass UL, though fortified foods and supplements can.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.statement: The AMDR is recommended for establishing DRIs for macronutrients in the future.
items.explanation: The CDRR method, not AMDR, was recommended for future DRI establishment.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 2)

items.statement: The energy needs of older persons tend to be higher than those of younger persons.
items.explanation: Energy needs of older persons are lower due to decline in lean tissue, physical activity, and health.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.statement: The RDAs apply only to oral intakes.
items.explanation: RDAs are specific for nutrients obtained through oral intake.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.statement: A universally recognized standard for nutrition screening is currently used in all health care facilities.
items.explanation: There is no single universally recognized or validated standard yet for nutrition screening.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.statement: Serum albumin levels <35 g/L may indicate a need for further nutritional assessment.
items.explanation: Abnormal serum albumin levels are nonspecific but can suggest the need for additional nutritional evaluation.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.statement: The Choose My Plate guide provides a web-based calculator to tailor servings for healthy individuals based on their weight, sex, age, and life-cycle stage.
items.explanation: Choose My Plate offers a web-based calculator that tailors the number of servings suggested for different demographics.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.statement: The ASA24 tool is used to assess physical activity levels in patients.
items.explanation: ASA24 is a dietary assessment tool, not for assessing physical activity.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.statement: The opinions expressed in this article reflect the views of the U.S. government.
items.explanation: The disclaimer states that the opinions are those of the author and do not reflect the views of the U.S. government.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.subtopic: NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER
items.statement: The AIs for micronutrients are based on experimental evidence.
items.explanation: AIs for micronutrients are based on epidemiologic observations, not direct experimental evidence.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · NUTRITIONAL STATUS ASSESSMENT | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DISCLAIMER

items.assertion: Conditionally essential nutrients must be supplied by the diet for certain individuals.
items.reason: These individuals cannot synthesize adequate amounts of these nutrients due to genetic defects or developmental immaturity.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why conditionally essential nutrients are necessary in the diet for specific groups.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment

items.assertion: Energy intakes are usually calculated from formulas rather than measuring TEE directly.
items.reason: Estimates introduce considerable error when calculating energy intake.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why calculations are used instead of direct measurements.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Protein needs decrease during growth and pregnancy.
items.reason: Increased energy intake can reduce protein requirements.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; protein needs increase during growth, pregnancy, lactation, and rehabilitation.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · ESSENTIAL NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: The DRIs include the Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDAs).
items.reason: RDAs are used to plan diets for individuals and ensure nutrient requirements for nearly all healthy persons.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why RDAs are included in DRIs.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.assertion: The EAR is used as a standard for food labels.
items.reason: Food labels use the RDA, not the EAR, to express nutrient content as a percentage of daily value.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the EAR is not used on food labels; instead, the RDA or its variant (DV) is used.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY REFERENCE INTAKES AND RDAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Iron absorption may be impaired by large amounts of calcium or lead.
items.reason: Calcium and lead can interfere with the uptake of iron in the digestive tract.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.assertion: Osteoporosis is solely caused by calcium deficiency.
items.reason: Calcium deficiency leads to weakened bones and osteoporosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; osteoporosis has multiple risk factors, not just calcium deficiency.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · FACTORS ALTERING NUTRIENT NEEDS

items.assertion: Dietary assessment in acute-care settings should focus on what patients are currently eating.
items.reason: Observations of food intake and patient willingness to eat provide insights into potential dietary inadequacies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why focusing on current eating habits is important.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: Most therapeutic diets in hospitals meet individual nutrient requirements.
items.reason: Hospital meals are designed to align with the Recommended Dietary Allowance if consumed as intended.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why most hospital diets aim to meet nutrient needs.
items.reference: Nutrient Requirements and Dietary Assessment · DIETARY ASSESSMENT (part 1)
